sentences.text: And so I'm leaving Svaty Liptovsky Mikulas on my way to Kosice, which is a town on the border of Hungary.
A smuggler there, who was guiding already a group of people together with me and the others which I met up in Svaty Liptovsky Mikulas.
There was probably like, oh, 10, 20, of us.
I don't remember the exact number.
Oh, well, he was guiding us, trying to help us so that we enter Hungary without being caught by the police.
Because, obviously, we didn't have any visas or passports and all that.
So we kind of smuggled through the border.
But before we knew, a Hungarian policeman jumps up from the bushes, waiting for us.
Somebody tipped him off.
Maybe that smuggler himself worked with the police and so forth.
And we were arrested right there and then.
And we were put in to-- The whole group of people?
The whole group, we were put into a local jail.
I still remember the jail was extremely clean, which I appreciated it very much.
Just a brand new building of a jail, right there in Kosice waiting for the train.
And then we were loaded on trains, you know, with-- with a crate with, you know, bars, reinforced bars the way you could-- you-- you convey criminals, you know.
And we came to Budapest.
This was a big jail, very dirty, very lousy, very old building.
The building was build by Maria Theresa.
It was scary at first a little bit.
But on--
I was with my father.
And--
No, we had different papers and different names, you see.
But we were together.
And we were put into a jail, into a cell, where there was-- there was probably near 100 people in that cell, anywhere from 80 to 100 people.
And those were people from all nationalities.
There were Russians, Estonians, Slovakians, Hungarians-- later on, a Pole came-- Romanians, people from all nationalities.
And during the day-- at night, the beds would be taken out.
The beds were in a corner.
During the night, they would spread out, and the whole floor was filled with beds, you know.
It was actually not that bad.
But the net, a steel net which acted as a mattress, and that's what you slept on.
In the morning, you would collect all that.
Wipe the floor clean, you know, and all day you could do-- you walked in the cell back and forth, back and forth, and talk to different people.
I got to know a lot of people, very interesting people.
I found it very exciting to-- to hear and to listen to them.
And in talking to them, I uncovered very quickly that I probably won't get killed, that the Hungarians, even if they take you as a Jew, they send you to labor.
But they don't kill.
No Auschwitz-- in other words, it was an entirely new set of conditions.
And of course, that was highly encouraging.
And once a day, we would have a walk.
And while walking in the jail, there was another group of people walking.
They were Jews.
And just across the fence was a different yard.
Across the fence, I got to talk to them a little bit.
And they told me that we can talk more if I slip in into their group.
And I'll get back to them into-- to-- their cell.
And then, they'll show me how to get back to the jail where I am.
So I talked to them.
I found out from them the story about-- I didn't tell them that I'm Jewish.
I didn't want to burden them with keeping secrets, you know.
And they told me that, again, no-- no-- no killing, no murders, you know.
It's bad enough that they have to, you know, sort of labor camps, and things were much worse.
They're now much better, and they are getting better and all that.
And I got interrogated by a-- they called him prosecutor.
But actually, he was like a-- no, he was actually like an investigative judge.
That was the name-- investigative judge.
So they brought you to him, and I would-- he was very friendly.
And-- and I could see that things will end up good for me.
As a matter of fact, he was kind of asking me questions in a way where he was tipping me of what answers I should give so that it will help me to get released from jail.
Well, he would kind of, you know, I don't remember the details.
But I knew right there and then that I'm-- I'm in good shape.
Now, my father came back from the interrogation, tell me the same story.
He believed that I'm a Pole.
He believed my father was a Pole.
He believed my father his name.
He believed me his name.
Didn't question, didn't interrogate, actually.
He kind of tried to help, you know.

Later on, I read an editorial in the Budapest paper which was maybe like in the months of January, where the editor of that paper-- which was in German, but it was one of the most important papers in-- in Budapest-- appealed to the population to go out of their way, and to the Budapest government, to help refugees and other people.
In order so that after the war, we can claim, you know, that we helped rather than, you know, pay for our sins which we did by cooperating with Germany officially.
Unfortunately, very quickly after that, Eichmann arrived.
OK, now, while I was in jail, there was a Polish person there who was caught in Yugoslavia on the border between Hungary and Yugoslavia.
And he was caught with very incriminating photos documenting the fact that he worked for the British CID, which is the British CIA, British intelligence service.
And he came back to the cell.
Unlike me and my father, he was beaten severely.
He was beaten on his feet.
And they insisted-- and it wasn't that same judge.
It was another guy who interrogated him.
And they insisted on him confessing that he is a spy for the British.
And he told me that he is finished, that he'll probably they'll kill him right there.
And he'll finish his life.
And I really pity on him.
And I, at that point, uncovered already a weakness in the system, where I felt that the person could escape their whole system.
The weakness was very simple.
They had a system where people who came to visit prisoners, families who came to visit prisoners, would come to the prison, register themselves in the book, give a name.
And this particular prisoner, who they came to visit, would be called out from his cell.
All those prisoners would be brought to a big yard.
The families who were visiting them would be entering that same yard.
Then the visit would be over.
Families would go back, and the prisoners would go back in the cells.
The families would not be released from the jail yard until every prisoner was recorded as having returned to his cell Then the families would be released to go back to their homes.

Now, that system though implied to me very quickly that if you, for some reason, managed to get out of your cell and get into the yard without anybody from the outside having called for you, they will never know.
You can escape jail that way, see?
And I arranged that with him.
I told him, look, when I go out of jail, I'll come.
I'll register myself that I'm trying to visit so and so, one of our friends who is still in jail.
He-- you, in the morning, will arrange a game with another.
The jail was run by-- by orderlies, you know.
And that was easy for him to arrange, that he would call you that particular day for labor.
Instead of going for that labor, you will hide.
There is a pile of wood there outside.
You'll hide in that pile of wood.
As the prisoners are called out from jail, you'll slip into that group.
You'll be brought into the yard, you see.
In the meantime, I'll come to visit this other prisoner.
But I'll meet you.
I'll have a coat, a spare coat, and I'll have a tie.
I'll put the tie on you.
I'll slip a coat on you.
You will leave together with me.
When that prisoner is entered back to jail, all of us will be released from jail.
The-- the system worked perfectly.
He walked out of jail together with me.
[LAUGHS]
See?
And I remember like today.
There was a big crowd of prisoners and families, and the guards on the outside.
And I slipped that coat on him.
And I took out the second tie.
And he put on the tie and tied it.
And I brought a hat.
And he put on the hat on his head.
And we walked out of jail.
See, there was like 300 or 400 prisoners, 300 and 400 family members, all in one yard, tight pack.
And the guards only on the outside watching everybody was there.
Now, my father left the jail together with me.
We were released more or less at the same time, together with others from our group.
Now, I, as a result of my-- of this escapade, this person was really working for the British intelligence.
And he arranged for me to enter the intelligence.
And I got an assignment.
I got a job, very well paid, very well paid.
And also, I got as a result of this, a paper where the Swiss consulate asks anybody who wants to arrest me or do something to counter them, that I'm sort of under them.
Not a Swiss citizen, but sort of under their protection, this type of paper I got, which gave me a lot of confidence.
And the job involved basically sitting in front of a window and recording the trains which were passing through, recording a tank, a cannon, a gun, soldiers, whatever.
There was a scheme, and I had to fill out that scheme.
And I did the job for-- since from about October when I was released from jail till May of 1944, for about three, eight months.
It paid very well.
Now, I lived-- I lived at different places.
And I've lived in different places.
But finally, I rented one house.
It was a Jewish elderly lady.
And the reason I bring that up because that's sort of a little interesting.
She was like 65 or maybe toward 70.
And she was in hard times.
And she was looking to rent somebody the big apartment, to rent somebody part of that apartment.
And one day, she invites me for tea and all that.
And then another time for tea, and I sit down there, and I listen to her.
And she had to wear this yellow star, the poor lady.
And she tells me, you know, all of this is only because of Polish Jews.

In other words, she fell to that propaganda which the Hungarians were trying to trumpet, that if weren't for those Polish Jews who behaved that badly, who came to Hungary after the First World War, this would have never happened to the Jews.
And she just repeats that line to me.
[LAUGHS] So-- but this is now May of 1940-- this-- no, March in 1944.
In March of 1944, Eichmann suddenly arrives into Hungary.
And while the first day, nothing happened, but I was immediately alerted.
I felt terribly bad things are going to happen.
And to the extent, my father had more contact with Hungarian Jews.
I talked to him, and I would visit him sometimes.
And we would talk to Hungarian Jews.
There were not too much close friendships with Hungarian Jews.
Somehow they were very aloof, maybe because of that propaganda, Polish Jews or whatever.
But anyhow, to the extent that we met people and could talk to them, we tried to tell them.
Look, something has to be done.
We have to do something, because what Eichmann-- once Eichmann is here-- when Eichmann came, the first thing he did, he arrested Horthy.
Horthy was the ruler of Hungary, the president so to speak.
And he was trying very much to be benevolent to Jews at that time in 1943, to reassure them, and nothing would happen to you.
And we regret what we did in 1941.
Because at first the Hungarians were very bad to the Jews.
And also, there was-- I forgot the name-- there was a guard there in Hungary, which were very anti-Semitic, Nazi-like sort of.
Horthy was fighting them and preventing them from gaining too much power.
Suddenly when Eichmann came, they got all the power they wanted.
And Horthy was basically put into house arrest.
So it was written on the wall.
Things are going to be very bad.
And indeed, they were later very bad for Hungarian Jews.
But what I found very interesting that when we tried to tell Hungarian Jews about it, nobody wanted to believe us.
They turned away.
You know, you're spreading propaganda.
You're scaring people away, this type of stuff.
And I immediately went to look, somehow, how to get out of Hungary and go to Romania.
I wasn't sure that Romania was much better, but my theory was simply it can't get worse than it's going to get in Hungary.
Eichmann is here.
That was enough.
Eichmann at that time was not known as yet as he's known now, because all what he did was not known.
But the fact that he-- that Horthy got arrested and put into house arrest, and the fact that he issued immediately ordered the Jews had to wear a yellow star.
There was too many signs which brought back signals that it's going to be bad.
And so I started to look again somehow for a smuggler who would help me to get across to Romania.
And I found one.
And I left Hungary in May of 1945.
Now, I have to mention that before leaving Hungary, there was the so-called [NON-ENGLISH] which is now very well.

Eichmann took two Jewish leaders, Zionist leaders and gave them a private plane, and sent them on his plane to Turkey to negotiate with [NON-ENGLISH] who was sort of like a foreign minister, Hungary's number two, to negotiate releasing Jews for trucks.
Now, while this story I won't get into that, because this is a long chapter which is what happened to them and so forth and so forth.
But many Jews like myself had those big hopes that heard Brand flew with the plane, he would come back, and we will allow somehow be released from Germany.
But I didn't believe in that.
Many Jews did, including, I have an uncle, who was also in Hungary at that time, who came after I came.
Only because the same way he found out from earlier.
And he told me I'm making a big mistake going to Romania because I'm risking myself, and this is almost sure that Eichmann is negotiating.
You know Brand was sent to Turkey with a private plane?
And can you imagine that the Jews will not be willing to collect money to give Hitler a few of these lousy trucks so that-- to save Jews?
I mean, it was impossible to imagine.
And I told him I didn't care what it is.
He said it was strictly my guess.
Of course, I didn't know much.
And I went to Romania.
And again, I went to a town, jumped out from the train across the town.
The smuggler showed me the way how to get through Romania.
And as soon as I reached the other side, I was arrested, the same thing as happened to me in Budapest.
This time, I was arrested, and before long, I was brought into a jail in a town which is called Belince.
The Romanian police.
At first, it was a peasant who panicked sort of.
He saw strange faces.
He passed me, and I could see him run quickly.
And he ran and alerted the soldiers, the police.
And the police came and arrested us.
And took us to this town, they put us there in jail.
Luckily--
I was, at that point, with a group mostly people that I didn't know, that this smuggler sort of put together, you know.
I was with my father.
And that was the only person I knew.
And I was together maybe with 10 other people that I met for the first time with that smuggler who took us over, you know.
And here we were in that jail.
And in that jail, there was 20 others who were put together.
So at that point there was like 30 of us in the jail.
But there was a Jewish community in that town.
And the first time they came over, they said, don't worry.
This guy is in our pocket.
He gets constantly money from us.
Don't worry.
We'll take care of you.
You'll be released.
A week passed by.
Two weeks passed by, and then, they came and gave us the bad news.
Unfortunately, somebody got interested in that case too much, and he lost his power.
He doesn't have the decision anymore, so he's afraid to release you.
He's responsible for you.
And looked very bad, because they finally officially-- and really, the fact of later, we were supposed to be brought back to the border.
But not to the Hungarian border, but to the Romanian-Polish border, and handed over to the Gestapo.
And the night before we left, the women of that town, really, they made a big dinner, brought a big cake, a chocolate cake.
I remember that like they had fish and everything.
And came to jail and cried and cried, they cried and cried.
It was a terribly emotional scene.
And the next day morning, we got up.
And we went on our way back to the border.
And that was a long journey because we were close to the Hungarian border, but quite far from the Polish border.
And by foot, each day we would walk like maybe, oh, 10 miles or 15 miles, whatever.
Go to another jail.
Sleep overnight.
Get up in the morning, go another 15 miles.
Go to another jail, sleep, and so forth.
Yeah.
But each time at that jail, the-- the jailer's wife would cheat us.
One was, as a matter of fact, so mean, she had pigs there.
And she was showing us-- the good food, I leave for the pigs.
But for you Jews, that's enough.
But that was only once.
But most of the time we were fed lousy food, but fed.
And just kept dragging and dragging, and got finally to the border.
Now, the Jews, when we left, told us we haven't given up.
And indeed, we came to the border.
And the last night, this was like 6 o'clock in the evening, did the guards changed.
And a new soldier came, a young soldier.
I never saw him before.
He came, and he took us.
And he didn't say a word.
He brings us to the border.
And he says, listen, you-- I will give a shot in the air, which is the signal I'm supposed to alert the German side to come, and come and pick you up.
But you, as soon as you hear me giving that shot, you turn around and you go this way, and you run this way.
And gave us exactly the description how to go to a village, and gave us a description in that village of a house where there was a Jew.
And he's waiting for you.
And this must have been prearranged [SOB] [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And really, it's-- it's-- I'm crying when I hear that.
And this was prearranged by those Jews back in-- [CRYING] And that's how it happened we came to that house.
And there was-- among us was one girl, and she had three children that she kind of adopted.
They were from ghettos that they-- that they-- they managed to run and come to Romania.
And they somehow survived in Romania, and were finally caught or whatever also this way.
No, they were-- must have been Hungarian, but I never met these people before.
I never met this girl before.
She was Romanian.
She spoke Romanian.
How she got into this group, I don't even know, because that's just the first time we met them, you know.
And this guy gave that shot.
We turned around and went.
And this was started like, at 6:00, 7:00 o'clock at night.
We reached that house from his description.
It must have been the middle of the night.
We knocked on the door.
The guy came out.
Yes, I'm waiting for you.
[SOB] The next day, he took us in his truck and took us to Arad, which was a large town, relatively large town-- many Jews, a Jewish community.
And they were free at that time.
They were-- you know.
And I stayed in Arad.
I must have-- this must have been already June, say, because I left in May.
It must have been June.
I stayed in Arad for a whole month.
And because I remember I had a girlfriend, with whom I spoke Hebrew, you see?
I used-- I knew fluent Hebrew from my school years in Tarnow.
And she learned Hebrew, you know.
She, altogether, she spoke Hebrew, she spoke French, and she spoke English, and she spoke German, fluent all the four languages, fluent-- very intelligent girl.
And you know, we were friends and all that, and stayed in Arad.
And the idea was that we can not stay Arad forever because it's too little a town and all that.
And arrangements were being made to help us get to Bucharest.
And they did make those arrangement, and we got to Bucharest.
Well, the Jewish community-- in Arad, there was already-- already a little bureaucracy.
There was a president in the Jewish community and all that, and I don't know exactly their arrangements.
But we were taken care of.
We were treated as, you know.
And we came to Bucharest, and somebody else took us over.
And there was a house.
And we lived all together in one big room and all this.
That was-- I arrived in Bucharest in July of 1944.
I left-- that's what I'm sure is I left-- Eichmann arrived in Hungary in March of 1944.
I left in May of 1944.
In about June, I was on the border back, you know-- in June, I arrived in Arad.
And I stayed in Arad the month, so sometimes July of 1944.
And I arrived in Budapest-- and things in Budapest looked great.
As a matter of fact, amongst ourselves, we said this is Jerusalem.
We walk the streets freely.
No yellow--
Bucharest-- no yellow star.
The Jews are free.
We would go to restaurant and meet Jews, no yellow star.
Everybody is free.
They told us how bad it was.
They used to be treated very harshly.
There were labor camps.
They would be beaten.
They would be tortured.
Many of them were killed.
Many of them died.
But this all suddenly changed.
See, the King, he was a collaborator of Hitler in the early years.
And he treated them very badly to show off before Hitler that he is, you know, a true Nazi and all that.
But now, when he saw Hitler as losing the war-- I mean, that Hitler is losing the war in 1944 in July was obvious to everybody except Hitler himself.
But everybody else realized that he lost the war.
This was where-- see-- see, the Allies learned that June 5 in Omaha, you know, the Normandy beaches.
That was June 5, 1944.
And this is a full month after that.
After Stalingrad, the Russians moved up there in Poland now.
The Russians already all through Poland.
They are on to [NON-ENGLISH].
So Germany is being crushed from all sides.
And the Allies are pounding in Normandy and moving towards Paris.
And that was the Normandy landing succeeded.
At first, it wasn't so sure it will succeed.
So Hitler lost the war to everybody except to himself and to Eichmann, you know.
So anyhow, here I am in Bucharest.
Everything is nice and dandy.
And suddenly, I get bounced on the street by two secret agents, and I get arrested.
Some group in the German-- in the Romanian police, wanted to prove, you see, that what we really are, that we are refugees from Nazi concentration camps.
And that they were baloney, but the-- but the papers were saying there, you know, what the Romanian Jews concocted to say about us.
And they grabbed me to prove the point.
Now, somebody from within my group must have somehow denounced or said something, or whatever.
And here I am again taken to the big jail Romania, a real giant building with the police headquarters.
And what a bureaucracy there, it is unbelievable the number of policemen.
And I'm constantly being interrogated.
And the fact of my circumcision becomes an issue.
I try to prove that I'm Jewish, you know.
And I have all the proof in the world, including-- And they keep sending me to doctors back and forth.
And because of these scars, the doctors, the bastards, say it's inconclusive.
See?
So next, I'm trying to tell them, look, you give me an exam.
It's impossible that somebody will know so much about Jewish prayers and the Talmud and the Torah and all that, that he has suddenly picked this up in a month.
It's only in a lifetime of study you can know of that.
And if that-- now, I was being interrogated by a guy who speaks fluent Polish and is about 60 years old.
And he keeps telling me, look, I had a son which is exactly looking like you at your age, and I lost him.
And you remind me of my son, and I want to save you.
If you just tell the truth, nothing will happen to you.
I didn't trust him.
I could sense that this is a come on, you know.
And that kept going on and on and on.
And in the meantime--
Well, where were you here?
Where were you there?
Trying to punch holes in my story-- I basically was telling the truth.
See, trying to, for the first time after so many years, I was telling them who I am, and really, the truth, you know.
But they wouldn't buy that.
And they were trying to punch holes in that, you know.
And at the same time, a guy from the Jewish community, a lawyer who also visited with me in jail, you see, and he was telling me-- look, never mind this interrogation.
We'll do something for you.
We'll bribe.
I mean, Romania, everything is by bribes.
We'll bribe.
We'll get you out.
Then one day, he comes, and he tells me, we can't bribe.
But we have another solution for you.
There is a boat leaving for Turkey.
We will simply-- we-- we got an agreement that we can get you on the boat, provided you disappear from this Earth.
Otherwise, the guy, who was-- who was being bribed is afraid.
See, he release you, and then you get rearrested.
And then he go to jail for having arranged your release.
But once you get on the boat, you disappear from this Earth, and you'll be all right.
[LAUGHS] And I got myself on that boat.
Yeah, I made one condition.
I'm not going on the boat unless my father goes with me.
They said, we'll take your father also.
Now, this wasn't so easy.
Everybody on that boat from Romanian Jews paid a lot of money, millions, to get on that boat.
And we are shipped.
We travel by train to stunts We get on that boat.
The name of the boat, Bulbul.
And there were three boats.
You got tapped on the shoulder.
[LAUGHS]
Now, the name of the boat is Bulbul, and there are three boats.
One is called Mefkure.
One is called Morina.
One is called Bulbul.
Before we leave the boat, two things happened which are extremely important.
Turkey was neutral.
Suddenly, Turkey announced that it leaves the neutrality, and it joins with the Allies in fighting Hitler.
That spelled great danger for going with that boat.
As a matter of fact, logically, I realize that.
Logically, I realize that I should get out of that boat.
But in my case, it was very difficult because of the jail story.
Second, nobody else left the boat.
And the reason they didn't leave the boat, it was so difficult to get on that boat.
You are already on the boat.
Ah, again, you like to believe every thing will be all right.
Then at 5 o'clock in the evening, when it was still light, a German submarine suddenly arrives in front of the three boats.
And the guy with binoculars, the captain with binoculars, gets on top of the deck there of that submarine and views the three boats, each one specifically.
